nodes.name: Wars & Conflicts
nodes.description: Major wars and military conflicts throughout world history

nodes.name: Historical Empires
nodes.description: Great empires that shaped world history

nodes.name: Major Revolutions
nodes.description: Political, social, and industrial revolutions that transformed societies

nodes.name: 20th Century World Events
nodes.description: Defining events of the 20th century and early 21st century

items.prompt: Which war (431–404 BCE) pitted Athens against Sparta and ended with the defeat of the Athenian Empire, described in detail by historian Thucydides?
items.explanation:
Athens (Delian League) vs. Sparta (Peloponnesian League), 431–404 BCE
Thucydides wrote *History of the Peloponnesian War* — the definitive ancient account, a model of historical methodology
A plague struck Athens in 430 BCE, killing ~25% of the population including the statesman Pericles
Sparta won with Persian financial support; Athens was forced to demolish its Long Walls and disband its fleet
Victory weakened all Greek city-states, enabling Macedonian domination under Philip II and Alexander the Great
Thucydides identified Spartan fear of growing Athenian power as the true cause — a dynamic later scholars call the 'Thucydides Trap'
items.cardFront: Athens vs. Sparta, 431–404 BCE

items.prompt: What name is given to the series of religious wars (1096–1291) launched by European Christians to recapture the Holy Land from Muslim rule, resulting in the sack of Constantinople in 1204?
items.explanation:
Nine major Crusades launched 1096–1272; broader Christian-Muslim conflict in the Holy Land continued until 1291
First Crusade (1096–1099) succeeded in capturing Jerusalem; other crusades largely failed or were diverted
Third Crusade (1189–1192): Richard I of England vs. Saladin; ended in stalemate with Saladin retaining Jerusalem
Fourth Crusade (1202–1204) infamously sacked Christian Constantinople — diverted by Venetian commercial interests
Knight orders founded during the Crusades: Knights Templar, Knights Hospitaller, Teutonic Knights
Crusades fostered the transfer of Islamic science, mathematics, and philosophy to medieval Europe
items.cardFront: Holy Land, European Christians vs. Muslims, 1096–1291

items.prompt: Which conflict (1337–1453) was fought between England and France over the French throne, famously turned by a teenage French peasant girl who claimed to hear divine voices?
items.explanation:
England vs. France, 1337–1453 (actually 116 years, fought intermittently)
Trigger: English king Edward III claimed the French throne via his French mother, Isabella
Joan of Arc (1412–1431) led French forces to key victories before being captured and burned at the stake as a heretic by the English-allied Burgundians
Ended with France expelling England from all continental territory except Calais
English longbow dominated early battles: Crécy (1346) and Agincourt (1415), where Henry V defeated a far larger French army
Aftermath in England: the dynastic instability sparked the Wars of the Roses (1455–1487) between Lancaster and York
items.cardFront: England vs. France, Joan of Arc, 1337–1453

items.prompt: Which devastating Central European conflict (1618–1648), begun by the Defenestration of Prague, transformed from a religious war into a broader power struggle and ended with the Peace of Westphalia?
items.explanation:
1618–1648 in the Holy Roman Empire; began as Catholic vs. Protestant conflict before drawing in all major European powers
Triggered by the Defenestration of Prague (1618): Protestant Bohemian nobles threw Catholic Habsburg officials from a castle window
France (Catholic) backed the Protestant side to weaken the Habsburgs — demonstrating the war's shift from religion to power politics
Peace of Westphalia (1648) established the principle of state sovereignty and religious tolerance — foundation of the modern nation-state system
Killed ~8 million people, mainly through famine and disease; Central European population declined 25–40%
Sweden emerged as a major power; France under Richelieu weakened the Habsburgs and became Europe's dominant land power
items.cardFront: 1618–1648, Defenestration of Prague, Peace of Westphalia

items.prompt: Which conflict (1756–1763), sometimes called 'the first world war,' saw Britain and Prussia defeat France, Austria, and Russia, with Britain gaining Canada and dominance in India?
items.explanation:
1756–1763; fought in Europe, North America, South America, West Africa, India, and the Philippines
Alliances: Britain + Prussia vs. France + Austria + Russia + Spain + Sweden
In North America called the French and Indian War; Britain gained Canada from France
British victory made it the world's dominant colonial power; France lost most of its overseas empire
Frederick the Great of Prussia survived against three great powers — cemented Prussia as a major European force
Britain's war debts led it to tax American colonists — a proximate cause of the American Revolution
items.cardFront: 1756–1763, Britain gains Canada, 'first world war'

items.prompt: Which series of conflicts (1803–1815) saw France under its emperor conquer most of continental Europe, ending in defeat at a famous battle in Belgium?
items.explanation:
France under Napoleon Bonaparte vs. shifting coalitions of European powers, 1803–1815
Napoleon conquered most of continental Europe and installed family members as rulers across Europe
Disastrous Russia campaign (1812): Grande Armée of ~600,000 entered Russia; fewer than 100,000 returned
Napoleon exiled to Elba (1814); escaped, raised a new army; defeated at Waterloo (June 18, 1815) by Wellington (Britain) and Blücher (Prussia)
Exiled to Saint Helena in the South Atlantic; died there in 1821, age 51
Congress of Vienna (1815) redrew the map of Europe and established a conservative balance of power lasting until WWI
items.cardFront: Napoleon Bonaparte, 1803–1815, Waterloo

items.prompt: Which conflict (1853–1856), fought primarily in the Black Sea region, saw Britain, France, and the Ottoman Empire defeat Russia, and is also famous for Florence Nightingale's nursing reforms?
items.explanation:
Britain, France, Sardinia, Ottoman Empire vs. Russia, 1853–1856; contested Russia's expansion at Ottoman expense
Florence Nightingale reformed military nursing at Scutari hospital, reducing mortality from ~42% to ~2% through sanitation
Charge of the Light Brigade (1854): disastrous British cavalry charge immortalized by Tennyson's poem
Russia lost; Treaty of Paris (1856) checked Russian expansion into the Black Sea region
First war with battlefield photography (Roger Fenton) and live telegraph reporting
Anesthetics and triage protocols developed during this war shaped modern military medicine
items.cardFront: 1853–1856, Crimean Peninsula, Florence Nightingale, Russia vs. Western Allies

items.prompt: Which war (1870–1871), triggered by Bismarck's manipulation of a diplomatic telegram, led to German unification under Prussian leadership and France losing Alsace-Lorraine?
items.explanation:
Prussia and German states vs. France, 1870–1871
Bismarck edited the Ems Dispatch to make it seem France had been insulted, provoking France into declaring war
Napoleon III captured at Sedan — humiliating French defeat; Paris besieged and fell
German Empire (Second Reich) proclaimed in Versailles' Hall of Mirrors (January 18, 1871) — deliberately staged inside France to humiliate it
France ceded Alsace-Lorraine to Germany; resentment fueled French nationalism until WWI
Paris Commune (March–May 1871): workers briefly seized control of Paris after France's surrender; suppressed with ~10,000 killed
items.cardFront: Prussia vs. France, 1870–1871, German unification, Bismarck

items.prompt: Which conflict (1904–1905), the first time a non-European power defeated a major European one in modern warfare, ended with a treaty mediated by U.S. President Theodore Roosevelt?
items.explanation:
Japan vs. Russia over Manchuria and Korea, 1904–1905
First major military victory by an Asian power over a European power in modern history
Japan destroyed Russia's Pacific Fleet at Port Arthur; then annihilated the Baltic Fleet after its 18,000-mile voyage at the Battle of Tsushima (1905)
Treaty of Portsmouth (1905) mediated by Theodore Roosevelt, who won the Nobel Peace Prize for it
Russia's humiliation contributed to the 1905 Russian Revolution and eroded Tsar Nicholas II's authority
Japan gained dominance over Korea, annexing it outright in 1910
items.cardFront: Japan defeats Russia, 1904–1905, Treaty of Portsmouth

items.prompt: Which global conflict (1914–1918), triggered by the assassination of Archduke Franz Ferdinand in Sarajevo, killed approximately 20 million people and ended four major empires?
items.explanation:
Allied Powers (UK, France, Russia, Italy, USA) vs. Central Powers (Germany, Austria-Hungary, Ottoman Empire, Bulgaria), 1914–1918
Sparked by assassination of Archduke Franz Ferdinand of Austria-Hungary in Sarajevo, June 28, 1914
First large-scale use of chemical weapons (chlorine/mustard gas), tanks (British invention, 1916), and aircraft in warfare
Gallipoli campaign (1915): failed Allied attempt to knock out the Ottoman Empire; ~130,000 Allied deaths
Treaty of Versailles (1919) imposed crushing reparations on Germany, sowing the seeds of WWII
Four empires fell: German, Austro-Hungarian, Ottoman, and Russian
items.cardFront: 1914–1918, Franz Ferdinand assassinated, four empires fell

items.prompt: Which conflict (1936–1939), won by Francisco Franco's Nationalists with Nazi and Fascist support, is commemorated in Picasso's painting *Guernica* and George Orwell's memoir *Homage to Catalonia*?
items.explanation:
Spanish Republic (Loyalists) vs. Nationalists under General Francisco Franco, 1936–1939
Nazi Germany and Fascist Italy aided Franco with troops and air power; USSR aided the Republic; International Brigades of foreign volunteers fought for the Republic
Guernica (April 26, 1937): Nazi Condor Legion bombed the Basque town; Picasso's painting became an enduring anti-war icon
George Orwell fought for the POUM militia and wrote *Homage to Catalonia* (1938) about the experience
Franco won; Spain officially remained neutral in WWII though Franco aided the Axis
Franco's regime lasted until his death in 1975 — making Spain one of Europe's last fascist dictatorships
items.cardFront: 1936–1939, Franco wins, Guernica, Orwell

items.prompt: Which global conflict (1939–1945), the deadliest in history with 70–85 million deaths, ended with the only wartime use of atomic weapons, dropped on two Japanese cities?
items.explanation:
Allied Powers (USA, UK, USSR, France, China) vs. Axis (Germany, Italy, Japan), 1939–1945
Deadliest conflict in history: ~70–85 million deaths, more than half of them civilians
Holocaust: Nazi Germany systematically murdered 6 million Jews and 5–6 million others (Roma, disabled, political prisoners, Soviet POWs)
D-Day (June 6, 1944): Allied landings at Normandy — the largest seaborne invasion in history
Germany surrendered May 8, 1945 (VE Day); Japan surrendered August 15, 1945 after atomic bombs on Hiroshima (Aug. 6) and Nagasaki (Aug. 9)
Nuremberg Trials (1945–1946) established the modern concept of crimes against humanity in international law
items.cardFront: 1939–1945, Holocaust, D-Day, Hiroshima/Nagasaki

items.prompt: Which conflict (1950–1953), known as 'the Forgotten War,' ended with an armistice leaving Korea divided near the same 38th parallel where it began?
items.explanation:
UN forces (mainly USA) and South Korea vs. North Korea and China, 1950–1953
North Korea invaded the South on June 25, 1950; UN forces under MacArthur drove them back to the Chinese border
China entered the war in October 1950, pushing UN forces back; war stabilized near the 38th parallel
President Truman fired General MacArthur in April 1951 for publicly advocating escalation against China
Armistice signed July 27, 1953; no formal peace treaty was ever signed — the Korean War is technically ongoing
Called 'the Forgotten War' — overshadowed by WWII and later Vietnam; ~36,000 American military deaths
items.cardFront: 1950–1953, North vs. South Korea, 38th parallel, 'Forgotten War'

items.prompt: Which conflict (1955–1975), in which the U.S. backed South Vietnam against Communist North Vietnam and the Viet Cong, ended with the fall of Saigon on April 30, 1975?
items.explanation:
North Vietnam (Communist) and Viet Cong vs. South Vietnam and USA, 1955–1975
Gulf of Tonkin incident (1964): alleged attack on USS Maddox used to justify large-scale U.S. escalation
Tet Offensive (January 1968): surprise coordinated attacks across South Vietnam during the Vietnamese New Year; turned American public opinion against the war
My Lai Massacre (March 1968): U.S. soldiers killed 300–500 unarmed South Vietnamese civilians
U.S. signed Paris Peace Accords (1973) and withdrew; Saigon fell April 30, 1975
~58,000 U.S. military deaths; 2–3 million Vietnamese deaths total, North and South
items.cardFront: 1955–1975, Tet Offensive, Fall of Saigon, Gulf of Tonkin

items.prompt: Which 1967 war saw Israel defeat Egypt, Jordan, and Syria in just six days, capturing the Sinai Peninsula, Gaza Strip, West Bank, East Jerusalem, and Golan Heights?
items.explanation:
Israel vs. Egypt, Jordan, Syria (plus Iraq, Saudi Arabia), June 5–10, 1967
Israel launched a preemptive air strike on June 5, destroying the Egyptian Air Force largely on the ground
In six days, Israel captured: Sinai and Gaza (from Egypt), West Bank and East Jerusalem (from Jordan), Golan Heights (from Syria)
Israel tripled its territory; the West Bank and Gaza remain occupied and contested today
Humiliating Arab defeat led to the Khartoum Declaration: 'no peace, no recognition, no negotiation'
Egyptian President Nasser resigned after the defeat, then retracted his resignation after public demonstrations
items.cardFront: Israel defeats Egypt/Jordan/Syria, June 5–10, 1967

items.prompt: Which 1973 conflict began with a surprise attack by Egypt and Syria on Israel on Judaism's holiest day, and led to a global oil embargo by Arab OPEC members against the United States?
items.explanation:
Egypt and Syria vs. Israel, October 6–25, 1973; launched on Yom Kippur, the holiest day of the Jewish calendar
Egypt successfully crossed the Suez Canal in force; Syria recaptured parts of the Golan Heights
Israel pushed back to near pre-war lines after massive U.S. emergency military resupply
Arab OPEC members imposed an oil embargo on the USA and its allies; oil prices quadrupled, triggering a global recession
Led to the Camp David Accords (1978) and Egypt–Israel peace treaty (1979) — the first Arab–Israeli peace deal
Sadat and Begin shared the 1978 Nobel Peace Prize; Sadat was assassinated by Egyptian Islamists in 1981
items.cardFront: Egypt/Syria surprise attack on Yom Kippur, 1973, oil embargo

items.prompt: Which 1982 conflict saw Britain send a naval task force 8,000 miles to recapture islands from Argentina, ending in British victory after 74 days of fighting?
items.explanation:
Britain vs. Argentina, April 2 – June 14, 1982
Argentina invaded and occupied the British-held Falkland Islands (called Islas Malvinas in Argentina)
Prime Minister Margaret Thatcher assembled a naval task force and sent it 8,000 miles south
~900 total deaths: 255 British, 649 Argentine, 3 Falkland Island civilians
Sinking of ARA General Belgrano (Argentine cruiser) remains controversial — it was outside the British exclusion zone
British victory boosted Thatcher's poll numbers; Argentina's defeat discredited its military junta, leading to a return to democracy in 1983
items.cardFront: Britain vs. Argentina, 1982, Falkland Islands, 74 days

items.prompt: Which 1990–1991 conflict saw a U.S.-led coalition of 35 nations liberate Kuwait from Iraqi occupation in Operation Desert Storm, with the ground campaign lasting just 100 hours?
items.explanation:
Coalition of 35 nations (led by USA) vs. Iraq under Saddam Hussein, August 1990 – February 1991
Iraq invaded Kuwait on August 2, 1990, claiming it was stealing oil via directional drilling and was historically Iraqi territory
Operation Desert Storm began January 17, 1991 with 5 weeks of aerial bombardment
Ground campaign (Operation Desert Sabre) lasted 100 hours; Kuwait liberated February 27, 1991
Iraq set fire to ~700 Kuwaiti oil wells on retreat, creating a months-long environmental disaster
First war extensively televised live (CNN); introduced the public to precision-guided 'smart bombs'
items.cardFront: Iraq invades Kuwait, 1990–1991, Operation Desert Storm, 100-hour ground war

items.prompt: Which 1992–1995 war in the former Yugoslavia included the Srebrenica massacre — the worst atrocity in Europe since World War II — and ended with the U.S.-brokered Dayton Agreement?
items.explanation:
Bosnia and Herzegovina vs. Republika Srpska (Bosnian Serbs, backed by Serbia), 1992–1995
Followed Bosnia's declaration of independence from Yugoslavia; Bosnian Serbs sought to create ethnically homogeneous territory
Siege of Sarajevo (1992–1996): 1,425 days — the longest siege of a capital city in modern warfare
Srebrenica massacre (July 1995): Bosnian Serb forces under Ratko Mladić killed ~8,000 Bosniak men and boys — ruled genocide by international courts
NATO air strikes broke the siege; Dayton Agreement (November 1995) ended the war
~100,000 people killed; 2.2 million displaced; first European genocide since the Holocaust
items.cardFront: 1992–1995, Yugoslavia, Srebrenica massacre, Dayton Agreement

items.prompt: Which 1980–1988 conflict, the longest conventional war of the 20th century, ended with no territorial changes and an estimated 500,000–1 million deaths after Iraq's invasion of its neighbor?
items.explanation:
Iraq (Saddam Hussein) vs. Iran (post-revolution Islamic Republic), September 1980 – August 1988
Iraq invaded Iran on September 22, 1980, hoping to exploit revolutionary chaos and seize oil-rich Khuzestan province
Iran repulsed Iraq's initial invasion; the war settled into trench warfare and use of human wave attacks
Iraq used chemical weapons against Iranian forces and Kurdish civilians; Halabja massacre (March 1988) killed ~5,000 Kurds
USA covertly supported both sides at different points; the Reagan administration provided Iraq with intelligence
Ceasefire August 1988; no territorial changes; ~500,000–1 million total deaths
items.cardFront: Iraq vs. Iran, 1980–1988, no territorial changes, chemical weapons

items.prompt: Which empire, the eastern successor to Rome lasting over 1,000 years, fell in 1453 when Ottoman Sultan Mehmed II captured its capital Constantinople?
items.explanation:
Eastern Roman Empire, 330–1453 CE; 'Byzantine' is a modern scholarly term — the empire called itself Roman throughout
Founded when Constantine I moved the imperial capital to Constantinople (ancient Byzantium) in 330 CE
Preserved Roman law, Greek culture, and Orthodox Christianity through the medieval period
Justinian I (r. 527–565) briefly reconquered much of the western Mediterranean and codified Roman law (Corpus Juris Civilis)
Fell May 29, 1453 when Mehmed II breached the walls with cannon; last emperor Constantine XI died fighting
Greek scholars fleeing to Italy after its fall are credited with helping ignite the Italian Renaissance
items.cardFront: Eastern Roman Empire, Constantinople, 330–1453 CE

items.prompt: Which Arab caliphate, ruling from Damascus, became the first great Islamic empire and at its early 8th-century peak extended from Spain and Portugal to Central Asia?
items.explanation:
661–750 CE; founded by Muawiya I after the first Islamic civil war; capital at Damascus
At its peak, one of the largest empires the world had yet seen: from Al-Andalus (Iberian Peninsula) to the Indus Valley
Defeated at the Battle of Tours/Poitiers (732) by Charles Martel, halting Muslim expansion into Western Europe
Instituted Arabic as the official administrative language; spread Islam across conquered territories
Overthrown by the Abbasid Revolution in 750 CE; surviving Umayyads fled to Spain and founded the Emirate of Córdoba
The Dome of the Rock in Jerusalem (completed 691 CE) is the oldest surviving Islamic monument, built under Umayyad rule
items.cardFront: First great Islamic empire, Damascus, 661–750 CE

items.prompt: Which Islamic caliphate, ruling from Baghdad, presided over the Islamic Golden Age of science and philosophy until the Mongols destroyed its capital in 1258?
items.explanation:
750–1258 CE; overthrew the Umayyads; founded Baghdad in 762 CE as the new capital
Presided over the Islamic Golden Age (8th–13th centuries): al-Khwarizmi invented algebra; Avicenna wrote the *Canon of Medicine*; al-Biruni measured the Earth's circumference
House of Wisdom (Bayt al-Hikma) in Baghdad: translated Greek, Persian, and Indian texts; preserved knowledge that reached medieval Europe via the Crusades
Harun al-Rashid (r. 786–809) corresponded with Charlemagne; his court inspired *One Thousand and One Nights*
Hulagu Khan (Genghis's grandson) sacked Baghdad in 1258; the caliph was executed; the House of Wisdom destroyed
Caliphate survived in exile in Cairo under Mamluk protection until the Ottoman conquest in 1517
items.cardFront: Baghdad, Islamic Golden Age, 750–1258 CE

items.prompt: Which empire, founded by Genghis Khan in 1206, became the largest contiguous land empire in history, stretching from the Pacific Ocean to Eastern Europe?
items.explanation:
Founded by Temüjin (Genghis Khan) in 1206 after unifying Mongolian tribes
Largest contiguous land empire in history: ~24 million km² at its peak — roughly the size of Africa
Genghis Khan's conquests killed an estimated 40 million people, possibly reducing world population by 10%
Pax Mongolica: once conquered, the empire enabled relatively safe trade and communication along the Silk Road
Divided among Genghis's successors into four khanates; Kublai Khan (grandson) founded the Yuan Dynasty in China
The Black Death (1347–1351) may have spread west along Mongol trade routes, killing ~1/3 of Europe's population
items.cardFront: Genghis Khan, founded 1206, largest contiguous land empire

items.prompt: Which West African empire controlled the gold and salt trade routes across the Sahara, and whose ruler Mansa Musa's 1324–1325 pilgrimage to Mecca reportedly crashed gold prices across the Mediterranean?
items.explanation:
West African empire c. 1235–1600; founded by Sundiata Keita after defeating the Sosso king at the Battle of Kirina
Controlled the gold and salt trade routes across the Sahara; one of the wealthiest civilizations of the medieval world
Mansa Musa (r. 1312–1337): his 1324–25 pilgrimage to Mecca with ~60,000 attendants and 12 tons of gold reportedly depressed gold prices across North Africa and the Middle East for a decade
Timbuktu: a major Islamic scholarly center; the Sankore Madrasah attracted scholars from across the Islamic world
Declined in the 15th century due to succession disputes and the rise of the Songhai Empire
Mansa Musa is frequently cited as the wealthiest person in recorded history in inflation-adjusted terms
items.cardFront: West Africa, Mansa Musa, gold and salt trade, Timbuktu

items.prompt: Which empire, founded by Osman I and centered on Constantinople/Istanbul, controlled much of Southeast Europe, West Asia, and North Africa for over six centuries before dissolving after World War I?
items.explanation:
Founded c. 1299 by Osman I in Anatolia; lasted until 1922 — over 600 years
Captured Constantinople in 1453 under Mehmed II; renamed Istanbul and made it the imperial capital
At its peak under Suleiman the Magnificent (r. 1520–1566), controlled three continents and 15–25 million people
Failed to capture Vienna twice: 1529 (Suleiman) and 1683 (Kara Mustafa) — the 1683 defeat marked the beginning of Ottoman retreat from Europe
Armenian Genocide (1915–1923): systematic killing of 600,000–1.5 million Armenians during WWI
Dissolved after WWI; Republic of Turkey proclaimed by Mustafa Kemal Atatürk in 1923
items.cardFront: 1299–1922, Istanbul, Suleiman the Magnificent, three continents

items.prompt: Which complex federation of Central European territories, lasting from 962 to 1806, prompted Voltaire's quip that it was 'neither holy, nor Roman, nor an empire'?
items.explanation:
962–1806 CE; a loose federation of kingdoms, principalities, and free cities in Central Europe, mostly German-speaking
Otto I crowned by Pope John XII in 962; modeled on Charlemagne's earlier Carolingian Empire
The emperor was an elected position — seven princes (Electors) chose the emperor, making it a constitutional monarchy of sorts
Voltaire quipped it was 'neither holy, nor Roman, nor an empire'
Peace of Westphalia (1648) ended the Thirty Years' War and fragmented the Empire into hundreds of autonomous states
Dissolved August 6, 1806 when Francis II abdicated after Napoleon's military victories; replaced by the Confederation of the Rhine
items.cardFront: Central Europe, 962–1806, Voltaire's 'neither holy, nor Roman, nor an empire'

items.prompt: Which empire, founded in 1492 with Columbus's voyage, became the first truly global empire controlling territory on five continents simultaneously, including most of the Americas and the Philippines?
items.explanation:
c. 1492–1898; began with Columbus's 1492 voyage sponsored by Ferdinand and Isabella
First truly global empire: controlled territory on five continents simultaneously at its peak
At its 16th-century peak under Philip II, included most of the Americas, the Philippines, parts of Africa and Italy
Silver from Potosí (Bolivia) fueled the Spanish economy and caused European-wide inflation ('Price Revolution')
Spanish Armada (1588): Philip II's 130-ship invasion fleet defeated by England and storms — signaled Spain's naval decline
Ended with the Spanish-American War (1898): Spain ceded Cuba, Puerto Rico, Guam, and the Philippines to the USA
items.cardFront: First global empire, Columbus 1492, Americas and Philippines

items.prompt: Which Muslim empire ruling most of the Indian subcontinent from 1526 to 1857 is best known for the Taj Mahal, built by Emperor Shah Jahan as a mausoleum for his wife Mumtaz Mahal?
items.explanation:
1526–1857; founded by Babur after the First Battle of Panipat; Babur was a descendant of both Timur and Genghis Khan
At its peak under Aurangzeb (d. 1707), controlled most of the Indian subcontinent — ~150 million people and ~25% of world GDP
Akbar the Great (r. 1556–1605): known for religious tolerance, administrative reforms, and cultural patronage; abolished the jizya tax on non-Muslims
Taj Mahal (1632–1653): built in Agra by Shah Jahan as a mausoleum for his wife Mumtaz Mahal; ~20,000 workers; designated a UNESCO World Heritage Site
British East India Company steadily encroached on Mughal territory; the Indian Rebellion of 1857 ended the empire
Last Mughal emperor Bahadur Shah Zafar II exiled to Rangoon (Yangon) by the British in 1858
items.cardFront: 1526–1857, Taj Mahal, India, Akbar the Great

items.prompt: Which Persian empire (1501–1736) converted Iran to Twelver Shia Islam, fundamentally shaping modern Iranian identity and creating the Sunni-Shia fault line that still defines Middle Eastern geopolitics?
items.explanation:
1501–1736; founded by Shah Ismail I, who conquered Persia and declared Twelver Shia Islam the state religion
Conversion of Iran to Shia Islam created the Sunni-Shia divide still central to Middle Eastern geopolitics today
Constant warfare with the Sunni Ottoman Empire (Ottoman–Safavid Wars, 1514–1639)
Shah Abbas I (r. 1588–1629): height of Safavid power; reformed the military, moved capital to Isfahan
Isfahan called 'half the world' (nesf-e jahan); its Naqsh-e Jahan Square is a UNESCO World Heritage Site
Fell to Afghan invasion in 1722; formally ended in 1736 when Nader Shah deposed the last Safavid
items.cardFront: Persia, 1501–1736, Shia Islam, rival of the Ottomans

items.prompt: Which last imperial dynasty of China (1644–1912), founded by the Manchu Aisin Gioro clan, reached its greatest territorial extent before declining under Western pressure and falling in the Xinhai Revolution?
items.explanation:
1644–1912; founded by the Manchu Aisin Gioro clan — ethnically distinct from the Han Chinese majority
At its 18th-century peak, controlled China, Mongolia, Tibet, Xinjiang, Taiwan, and parts of Central Asia
Opium Wars (1839–1860): Britain forced China to legalize opium imports; China ceded Hong Kong to Britain (1842) — inaugurating the 'century of humiliation'
Taiping Rebellion (1850–1864): internal civil war killed an estimated 20–30 million people — one of the deadliest conflicts in history
Boxer Rebellion (1900): anti-foreign uprising suppressed by a coalition of eight foreign nations
Ended with the Xinhai Revolution; last emperor Puyi (age 6) abdicated February 12, 1912
items.cardFront: Last Chinese dynasty, 1644–1912, Manchu rulers, Opium Wars

items.prompt: Which empire, at its early 20th-century peak controlling 24% of Earth's land area and 412 million people, was famously described as 'the empire on which the sun never sets'?
items.explanation:
c. 1583–1997; at its 1920s peak, the largest empire in history by area: ~24% of Earth's land surface
'The empire on which the sun never sets' — at any given moment, it was daytime somewhere in the empire
Began with Elizabethan privateers and the East India Company (founded 1600)
Abolished the transatlantic slave trade in 1807 (Slavery Abolition Act 1833 freed enslaved people throughout the empire)
Indian independence (1947) and the decolonization of Africa (1950s–1970s) dismantled the empire
Formal end: Hong Kong returned to China on July 1, 1997 — the last major colony
items.cardFront: Largest empire in history, 'sun never sets,' 1920s peak

items.prompt: Which empire, proclaimed by Peter the Great in 1721, grew into the world's largest country and a major European power before collapsing in the Russian Revolution of 1917?
items.explanation:
1721–1917; proclaimed by Peter the Great after victories in the Great Northern War against Sweden
At its peak, the world's largest country by area: 22.8 million km² (about 1/6 of Earth's land surface)
Peter the Great (r. 1682–1725): Westernized Russia, founded St. Petersburg as the new capital, built the first Russian navy
Catherine the Great (r. 1762–1796): expanded into Ukraine, Crimea, and Poland; corresponded with Voltaire and Diderot
Alexander II emancipated the serfs in 1861; was assassinated by revolutionary populists in 1881
Nicholas II abdicated March 2, 1917 after catastrophic WWI losses and the February Revolution; executed by Bolsheviks July 17, 1918
items.cardFront: Peter the Great, 1721–1917, world's largest country

items.prompt: Which empire, proclaimed in the Hall of Mirrors at Versailles in 1871 after Prussia's defeat of France, rapidly industrialized to become Europe's most powerful nation before collapsing after World War I?
items.explanation:
1871–1918; proclaimed January 18, 1871 in Versailles' Hall of Mirrors — deliberately staged inside France after its defeat
Unified under Prussian leadership by Otto von Bismarck, 'the Iron Chancellor'
Rapidly industrialized to surpass Britain in steel production and chemical industry by 1914
Wilhelm II dismissed Bismarck in 1890 and pursued aggressive naval and colonial expansion, stoking European tensions
Triple Alliance (Germany, Austria-Hungary, Italy) vs. Triple Entente (Britain, France, Russia) — the alliance structure that made WWI a world war
Kaiser Wilhelm II abdicated November 9, 1918; armistice signed November 11, 1918 (the 11th hour of the 11th day of the 11th month)
items.cardFront: 1871–1918, Bismarck, unified Prussia, Hall of Mirrors

items.prompt: Which 'dual monarchy' empire, formed in 1867 and ruled by the Habsburg dynasty, collapsed after World War I — the very war its assassination of Archduke Franz Ferdinand had helped trigger?
items.explanation:
1867–1918; formed by the Austro-Hungarian Compromise (Ausgleich) uniting Austria and Hungary under Habsburg rule
Multiethnic empire of ~50 million people speaking 11+ languages: Germans, Hungarians, Czechs, Poles, Croats, Slovaks, Serbs, and others
Emperor Franz Joseph I ruled 1848–1916 — nearly 68 years, one of history's longest reigns
Archduke Franz Ferdinand (heir to the throne) assassinated in Sarajevo on June 28, 1914, triggering WWI
Fought WWI as part of the Central Powers; defeated on multiple fronts
Dissolved November 1918; successor states included Austria, Hungary, Czechoslovakia, Yugoslavia, and parts of Poland, Romania, and Italy
items.cardFront: Habsburg dynasty, 1867–1918, multiethnic, Franz Joseph

items.prompt: Which nearly bloodless 1688 revolution in England overthrew the Catholic King James II and installed William of Orange and Mary II to rule under parliamentary constraints, establishing constitutional monarchy?
items.explanation:
1688; Parliament invited William III of Orange (Netherlands) and his wife Mary II to replace James II
James II was Catholic; Parliament feared a Catholic succession and the restoration of royal absolutism
'Glorious' because nearly bloodless — James fled to France without a major battle
English Bill of Rights (1689): codified Parliament's supremacy over the monarchy; barred Catholics from the throne
Established constitutional monarchy and parliamentary sovereignty as England's governing model
Locke's *Two Treatises of Government* (1689) provided the philosophical justification; directly influenced the American and French Revolutions
items.cardFront: 1688, William of Orange, bloodless, Bill of Rights

items.prompt: Which revolution (1775–1783), sparked by taxation without representation, produced the Declaration of Independence and created the world's first modern constitutional republic?
items.explanation:
Thirteen British colonies rebelled against British rule, 1775–1783
Declaration of Independence (July 4, 1776) drafted primarily by Thomas Jefferson, articulating Enlightenment natural rights theory
Battle of Saratoga (1777): turning point — convinced France to ally with the Americans, transforming a colonial revolt into a world war
George Washington commanded the Continental Army; Valley Forge (winter 1777–78) was the low point; soldiers starved and froze
Treaty of Paris (1783): Britain recognized American independence; USA gained all territory east of the Mississippi
U.S. Constitution (1789) and Bill of Rights (1791) created the template for modern constitutional democracy
items.cardFront: 1775–1783, Declaration of Independence, first modern republic

items.prompt: Which revolution (1789–1799), sparked by financial crisis and feudal inequality, produced the Declaration of the Rights of Man, guillotined a king, and culminated in Napoleon's rise to power?
items.explanation:
1789–1799; began with the storming of the Bastille on July 14, 1789 — still celebrated as Bastille Day
Declaration of the Rights of Man and of the Citizen (1789): foundational document of human rights; directly inspired by the American Declaration
Reign of Terror (1793–1794) under Robespierre: 16,000+ officially executed by guillotine; Robespierre himself guillotined in the Thermidorian Reaction
King Louis XVI guillotined January 21, 1793; Marie Antoinette guillotined October 16, 1793
Revolutionary ideals ('Liberté, Égalité, Fraternité') and the Napoleonic Code spread across Europe
Caused in part by France's near-bankruptcy from financing the American Revolution
items.cardFront: 1789–1799, Bastille, guillotine, Robespierre, Napoleon

items.prompt: Which revolution (1791–1804) produced the only successful slave revolt in history to found an independent nation, creating the world's first Black republic?
items.explanation:
1791–1804; enslaved people and free people of color in the French colony of Saint-Domingue (modern Haiti) rose up
Only successful slave revolt in history to result in a permanent independent nation
Toussaint Louverture led the revolution's military campaigns; captured by Napoleon's forces through treachery and died in a French prison in 1803
Jean-Jacques Dessalines proclaimed Haitian independence on January 1, 1804 — the world's first Black republic
Named Haiti from the indigenous Taíno word for 'land of high mountains'; second-oldest republic in the Western Hemisphere
France demanded 150 million gold francs (later reduced to 90 million) in reparations to compensate former slave-owners — payments continued until 1947, impoverishing Haiti for generations
items.cardFront: 1791–1804, only successful slave revolt, first Black republic, Toussaint Louverture

items.prompt: Which transformation (c. 1760–1840), beginning in Britain with the mechanization of textile production and James Watt's improvements to the steam engine, fundamentally changed how goods were made and how people lived?
items.explanation:
c. 1760–1840 in Britain; the first Industrial Revolution; a second phase occurred 1870–1914 globally
James Watt's improved steam engine (patented 1769) was applied first to factories, then to railways (Stephenson's Rocket, 1829) and ships
Mechanized textile production — spinning jenny, power loom, water frame — transformed Britain's cotton industry from cottage craft to factory system
Migration from rural to urban; child labor in mines and factories; coal became the dominant fuel
Britain's head start gave it global industrial dominance; by 1870 it produced ~20% of world manufacturing output
Sparked labor movements, trade unionism, and Marxism as responses to industrial working conditions
items.cardFront: c. 1760–1840, Britain, steam engine, mechanized production

items.prompt: Which wave of liberal and nationalist uprisings swept France, Austria-Hungary, the German states, and Italy in 1848 — the largest revolutionary wave in European history — and were commemorated in the nickname 'Springtime of Nations'?
items.explanation:
1848: simultaneous revolutions in France, German states, Austrian Empire, Italian states, Denmark, and more
France: February Revolution ousted Louis-Philippe; Second Republic proclaimed; Louis-Napoleon elected president, then made himself Emperor Napoleon III
Karl Marx and Friedrich Engels published *The Communist Manifesto* in February 1848, days before the revolutions
Most revolutions failed by 1849, crushed by conservative armies; but serfdom was abolished in Austria and Hungary
Nationalist movements failed but planted seeds: German and Italian unification achieved within 20 years
Revolutions of 1848 demonstrated the power of nationalist sentiment and liberal constitutionalism across Europe
items.cardFront: 1848, pan-European uprisings, 'Springtime of Nations', Communist Manifesto

items.prompt: Which twin revolutions of 1917 ended the Romanov dynasty with Tsar Nicholas II's abdication and then brought the Bolsheviks under Lenin to power, establishing the world's first communist state?
items.explanation:
Two revolutions in 1917: February Revolution (March by Gregorian calendar) and October Revolution (November by Gregorian calendar)
February Revolution: mass protests and military mutinies forced Nicholas II to abdicate March 2; Provisional Government under Kerensky took power
Lenin returned from Swiss exile in the 'Sealed Train' arranged by Germany, which hoped to destabilize Russia
October Revolution (November 7, 1917): Bolsheviks overthrew the Provisional Government in a near-bloodless coup; seized the Winter Palace in Petrograd
Russian Civil War followed (1918–1922): Reds (Bolsheviks) defeated Whites (anti-Bolshevik forces backed by Western powers)
Nicholas II and his entire family shot by Bolsheviks on July 17, 1918 in Yekaterinburg; remains identified by DNA in the 1990s
items.cardFront: 1917, Lenin/Bolsheviks, end of Romanov dynasty

items.prompt: Which revolution concluded on October 1, 1949 when Mao Zedong proclaimed the People's Republic of China from Tiananmen Gate, after defeating Chiang Kai-shek's Nationalist forces in a civil war?
items.explanation:
Chinese Civil War between Communists (CPC under Mao Zedong) and Nationalists (KMT under Chiang Kai-shek), 1927–1949
Long March (1934–1935): Mao's forces marched ~5,600 miles to escape Nationalist encirclement — Mao emerged as undisputed leader
Japanese invasion (1937–1945) temporarily united the two sides; Civil War resumed immediately after Japan's surrender
Mao proclaimed the People's Republic of China from Tiananmen Gate on October 1, 1949
Chiang Kai-shek and the Nationalist government retreated to Taiwan (still the Republic of China government today)
Established the most populous nation on Earth as Communist; led directly to U.S. involvement in Korea
items.cardFront: Mao Zedong, October 1, 1949, People's Republic of China

items.prompt: Which revolution succeeded on January 1, 1959 when Fidel Castro's guerrilla movement overthrew the U.S.-backed dictator Fulgencio Batista, establishing a Communist government 90 miles from Florida?
items.explanation:
Fidel Castro, Che Guevara, and Raúl Castro led a guerrilla war against Batista's dictatorship, 1953–1959
Began with the failed Moncada Barracks attack (July 26, 1953); survivors formed the 26th of July Movement
Castro led 82 rebels aboard the yacht *Granma* from Mexico to Cuba (December 1956); most were killed; survivors fled to the Sierra Maestra mountains
Batista fled on January 1, 1959; Castro's forces marched into Havana
Bay of Pigs invasion (April 1961): CIA-trained Cuban exiles failed to overthrow Castro — an embarrassment for the Kennedy administration
Cuba became a Communist state; Castro ruled until 2008; the revolution led directly to the Cuban Missile Crisis (1962)
items.cardFront: Fidel Castro, January 1, 1959, Communist Cuba

items.prompt: Which revolution in 1979 overthrew Shah Mohammad Reza Pahlavi, replacing Iran's pro-Western monarchy with an Islamic Republic under Ayatollah Khomeini and reshaping Middle Eastern geopolitics?
items.explanation:
Mass protests and strikes overthrew Shah Mohammad Reza Pahlavi in January–February 1979
Ayatollah Ruhollah Khomeini returned from exile in Paris on February 1, 1979 and established the Islamic Republic
Constitution created a Supreme Leader (Khomeini) with ultimate authority above elected institutions
Iran hostage crisis (1979–1981): 52 American diplomats held 444 days after students seized the U.S. Embassy in Tehran; freed moments after Reagan's inauguration
Iran went from a secular pro-Western monarchy to a Shia theocracy hostile to the USA and Israel overnight
Iran–Iraq War (1980–1988) followed immediately: Iraq invaded, fearing the revolution would spread to its Shia population
items.cardFront: 1979, Ayatollah Khomeini, overthrew the Shah, Islamic Republic

items.prompt: Which largely peaceful revolution in November 1989 ended Communist rule in Czechoslovakia, led by dissident playwright Václav Havel, who became the country's first post-Communist president?
items.explanation:
November 17 – December 29, 1989; peaceful transition from Communist to democratic rule in Czechoslovakia
Began with a student demonstration in Prague on November 17 violently suppressed by police; triggered mass national protests
'Velvet' because of its non-violent character — strikes and demonstrations alone brought down a regime of 40+ years
Václav Havel: playwright and dissident imprisoned under Communist rule; became Czechoslovakia's first post-Communist president on December 29, 1989
Part of the wider 1989 Revolutions: Poland, Hungary, East Germany, Bulgaria, and Romania all shed Communist rule that year
Czechoslovakia peacefully split into the Czech Republic and Slovakia on January 1, 1993 — the 'Velvet Divorce'
items.cardFront: 1989, Czechoslovakia, Václav Havel, peaceful end of Communism

items.prompt: Which wave of pro-democracy uprisings (2010–2012) swept the Arab world, sparked by a Tunisian street vendor's self-immolation, and toppled governments in Tunisia, Egypt, Libya, and Yemen?
items.explanation:
December 2010 – 2012; pro-democracy and anti-government protests across the Arab world
Sparked by Mohamed Bouazizi, a Tunisian fruit vendor who set himself on fire on December 17, 2010 after police confiscated his cart
Tunisia: President Ben Ali fled after 23 years; the only Arab Spring country to achieve lasting democratic transition
Egypt: President Mubarak resigned February 11, 2011 after 18 days of Tahrir Square protests; military eventually took power (al-Sisi)
Libya: Gaddafi captured and killed on October 20, 2011 after NATO-backed civil war; descended into prolonged instability
Syria: Assad refused to step down; civil war erupted in 2011, killing 400,000+ and displacing 13 million
items.cardFront: 2010–2012, pro-democracy uprisings, Arab world, Tunisia to Syria

items.prompt: Which economic catastrophe, triggered by the U.S. stock market crash of October 1929, spread globally to cause 25% unemployment in the USA, democratic backsliding across Europe, and is considered a contributing cause of World War II?
items.explanation:
1929–1939; the worst economic downturn of the 20th century
Black Tuesday (October 29, 1929): U.S. stock market crashed, erasing billions in paper wealth
U.S. unemployment reached 25% by 1933; GDP fell ~30%; 9,000 banks failed; millions of families lost homes and farms
Smoot-Hawley Tariff (1930): U.S. raised import tariffs; other nations retaliated; global trade collapsed by ~65%
FDR's New Deal (1933–1939): relief, recovery, and reform programs; created Social Security, the SEC, and the FDIC
Hitler exploited German economic desperation (unemployment ~30%) to rise to power — the Depression helped produce WWII
items.cardFront: 1929–1939, stock market crash, 25% unemployment, FDR's New Deal

items.prompt: What name is given to Nazi Germany's systematic, state-sponsored murder of six million Jews and millions of others during World War II, carried out in concentration and extermination camps?
items.explanation:
1941–1945; Nazi Germany and collaborators murdered ~6 million Jews — two-thirds of European Jewry
Also killed: ~5–6 million others — Roma, disabled people, Soviet POWs, political prisoners, homosexuals, Jehovah's Witnesses
Nuremberg Laws (1935) stripped Jews of German citizenship; Kristallnacht (November 9–10, 1938) was a nationwide pogrom
Wannsee Conference (January 1942): senior Nazi officials coordinated the 'Final Solution to the Jewish Question'
Major extermination camps: Auschwitz-Birkenau, Treblinka, Sobibór, Belzec, Chelmno, Majdanek — all in occupied Poland
Nuremberg Trials (1945–1946) prosecuted Nazi leaders; established 'crimes against humanity' as a concept in international law
items.cardFront: Nazi Germany, 1941–1945, 6 million Jews, systematic genocide

items.prompt: Which international organization, founded on October 24, 1945 with 51 original member states to replace the failed League of Nations, now has 193 members and is headquartered in New York City?
items.explanation:
Founded October 24, 1945; 51 original members; 193 members today
Succeded the League of Nations (1920–1946), which failed to prevent WWII — notably the USA never joined the League
Four principal organs: General Assembly, Security Council (5 permanent members with veto power: USA, UK, France, Russia, China), Secretariat, International Court of Justice
UN Charter drafted at the San Francisco Conference (April–June 1945)
Headquarters on the East River in Manhattan, on land donated by John D. Rockefeller Jr.
Specialized agencies include UNESCO, WHO, UNICEF, FAO, and the World Bank; peacekeeping forces deployed globally
items.cardFront: Founded 1945, 193 members, New York City headquarters

items.prompt: Which ideological and geopolitical rivalry between the USA and the Soviet Union (1947–1991), fought through proxy wars, the space race, and nuclear standoffs, ended with the dissolution of the USSR?
items.explanation:
1947–1991; USA (and NATO allies) vs. USSR (and Warsaw Pact) — the defining geopolitical conflict of the 20th century
Mutually Assured Destruction (MAD): both sides had enough nuclear weapons to destroy each other many times over; this deterred direct military conflict
Proxy wars fought on behalf of each side: Korea, Vietnam, Angola, Afghanistan, and across Latin America
Space Race: Sputnik (1957), Gagarin first in space (1961), Armstrong on the Moon (1969 — the American victory)
Berlin Wall (1961–1989): physical symbol of the Iron Curtain; at least 140 people killed attempting to cross
Ended with the dissolution of the Soviet Union on December 25, 1991
items.cardFront: USA vs. USSR, 1947–1991, nuclear deterrence, proxy wars

items.prompt: Which 1947 division of British India into independent India and Pakistan, drawn along religious lines, triggered one of the largest mass migrations in history and communal violence killing hundreds of thousands?
items.explanation:
August 14–15, 1947; British India split into two independent nations: Pakistan (Muslim majority) and India (Hindu/Sikh majority)
Cyril Radcliffe drew the border in just 5 weeks with limited local knowledge; his Radcliffe Line split Punjab and Bengal
10–20 million people displaced as Hindus, Sikhs, and Muslims fled to the 'right' side of the new borders
Estimated 200,000–2 million killed in communal massacres and revenge attacks during the migration
Mahatma Gandhi, who had campaigned for Hindu-Muslim unity, was assassinated on January 30, 1948 by a Hindu nationalist
Kashmir remains disputed between India, Pakistan, and China to this day; the partition's violence still shapes South Asian politics
items.cardFront: 1947, British India divided into India and Pakistan, mass migration, communal violence

items.prompt: Which system of institutionalized racial segregation enforced in South Africa from 1948 to 1994 was dismantled when Nelson Mandela, released after 27 years in prison, won the country's first multiracial election?
items.explanation:
'Apartheid' means 'separateness' in Afrikaans; enacted by the National Party government in 1948
Laws classified all South Africans as White, Coloured, Indian, or Black; dictated where each could live, work, and travel
ANC (African National Congress) led the resistance; Nelson Mandela imprisoned on Robben Island 1964–1990 (27 years)
International sanctions, cultural boycotts, and sporting bans isolated South Africa economically and diplomatically
F.W. de Klerk unbanned the ANC and released Mandela on February 11, 1990
Mandela and de Klerk shared the Nobel Peace Prize in 1993; Mandela elected South Africa's first Black president April 1994
items.cardFront: South Africa, 1948–1994, racial segregation, Nelson Mandela

items.prompt: Which state was established on May 14, 1948 when David Ben-Gurion read its Declaration of Independence, prompting the USA to extend recognition within 11 minutes and five Arab armies to invade the next day?
items.explanation:
David Ben-Gurion proclaimed the State of Israel in Tel Aviv on May 14, 1948 as the British Mandate for Palestine expired
President Truman extended U.S. de facto recognition within 11 minutes of the declaration
Five Arab armies (Egypt, Jordan, Syria, Iraq, Lebanon) invaded the next day; 1948 Arab-Israeli War ('Israeli War of Independence')
Israel survived and in armistice agreements (1949) held more territory than the 1947 UN partition plan had allocated
Palestinian refugee crisis: ~700,000 Palestinian Arabs fled or were expelled — called the Nakba ('catastrophe')
Zionist movement founded by Theodor Herzl in 1897; the Holocaust gave the cause international momentum
items.cardFront: David Ben-Gurion, May 14, 1948, declared in Palestine

items.prompt: Which 13-day confrontation in October 1962, when the USA discovered Soviet nuclear missiles in Cuba, brought the world closer to nuclear war than at any other point during the Cold War?
items.explanation:
October 16–28, 1962; 13-day standoff between the USA and USSR — the closest the Cold War came to nuclear exchange
U-2 spy plane photographs revealed Soviet missile sites under construction in Cuba
President Kennedy imposed a naval blockade ('quarantine') around Cuba; demanded removal of the missiles
Soviet ships carrying more missiles turned back from the quarantine line; Khrushchev agreed to remove the missiles
Secret deal: USSR removed Cuban missiles; USA secretly removed Jupiter missiles from Turkey and pledged not to invade Cuba
Hotline (direct telephone link) between Washington and Moscow established afterwards to prevent future miscommunication
items.cardFront: October 1962, Soviet missiles in Cuba, Kennedy vs. Khrushchev, 13 days

items.prompt: Which political campaign (1966–1976) launched by Mao Zedong mobilized Red Guards to purge 'counter-revolutionary' elements in China, closing universities, destroying cultural heritage, and killing hundreds of thousands?
items.explanation:
1966–1976; officially the 'Great Proletarian Cultural Revolution'; launched by Mao Zedong to reassert his authority within the Communist Party
Mao mobilized millions of young 'Red Guards' to attack the 'Four Olds': old customs, culture, habits, and ideas
Intellectuals, teachers, artists, and party officials were publicly humiliated, imprisoned, tortured, or killed; universities closed for years
Deng Xiaoping (future paramount leader) was purged twice; Lin Biao (Mao's chosen successor) died fleeing to the USSR after a failed coup attempt in 1971
Historical sites, temples, books, and artworks destroyed across China
Ended with Mao's death (September 9, 1976) and the arrest of the 'Gang of Four'; estimated 500,000–2 million deaths
items.cardFront: 1966–1976, Mao Zedong, Red Guards, destroyed Chinese cultural heritage

items.prompt: What historic event occurred on July 20, 1969 when Apollo 11 astronauts Neil Armstrong and Buzz Aldrin became the first humans to walk on the Moon, with Armstrong declaring it 'one small step for man'?
items.explanation:
July 20, 1969; Apollo 11 mission; lunar module *Eagle* landed in the Sea of Tranquility
Neil Armstrong: first human to walk on the Moon; said 'That's one small step for man, one giant leap for mankind' (he later stated he intended 'for a man')
Buzz Aldrin joined Armstrong on the surface; Michael Collins orbited in the command module *Columbia*
Fulfilled JFK's 1961 challenge to land on the Moon 'before this decade is out,' announced less than three weeks after Yuri Gagarin's first human spaceflight
Soviet Luna program had been racing to get there first; the USSR never landed humans on the Moon
Six Apollo missions successfully landed on the Moon (11, 12, 14, 15, 16, 17); last crewed mission was Apollo 17 (December 1972)
items.cardFront: July 20, 1969, Neil Armstrong, Apollo 11, Sea of Tranquility

items.prompt: Which event on June 4, 1989 saw Chinese military crush pro-democracy demonstrators in Beijing, killing hundreds to thousands, and is symbolized by an unidentified man standing alone before a column of tanks?
items.explanation:
June 3–4, 1989; the People's Liberation Army crushed student-led pro-democracy protests in Beijing
Protests began April 15 after the death of reformist leader Hu Yaobang; grew to ~1 million people in Beijing and spread to 400+ cities
Martial law declared May 20; the army ordered in on the night of June 3–4
Death toll disputed: Chinese Red Cross initially estimated 2,600; Chinese government claimed 241; leaked documents suggest the true figure is in the hundreds to thousands
'Tank Man' (June 5): an unidentified man stood in the path of a column of tanks — one of the 20th century's most iconic photographs
Heavily censored in China to this day; Western governments imposed sanctions, normalized within years
items.cardFront: June 4, 1989, Beijing, pro-democracy crackdown, Tank Man

items.prompt: Which event on November 9, 1989 ended the 28-year physical division of Berlin, as crowds spontaneously demolished sections of a barrier that had symbolized the Iron Curtain since 1961?
items.explanation:
November 9, 1989; East Germany announced citizens could cross freely; crowds spontaneously demolished sections of the wall
Berlin Wall built beginning August 13, 1961 to stop the mass exodus of East Germans to West Berlin (3.5 million had fled since 1945)
At least 140 people killed attempting to cross (official estimate; actual number may be higher); East German guards had shoot-to-kill orders
A miscommunication at a press conference — Günter Schabowski said citizens could travel 'immediately, without delay' — caused crowds to rush the checkpoints
German reunification followed on October 3, 1990
Part of the 1989 Revolutions that ended Communist rule across Eastern Europe in rapid succession
items.cardFront: November 9, 1989, end of 28-year division of Berlin

items.prompt: What occurred on December 25, 1991 when Mikhail Gorbachev resigned as president, the Soviet flag was lowered over the Kremlin, and the USSR formally ceased to exist after 69 years?
items.explanation:
December 25–26, 1991; Gorbachev resigned; Soviet flag replaced by the Russian tricolor over the Kremlin
Gorbachev's reforms — glasnost (openness) and perestroika (restructuring) — had loosened Communist Party control without saving it
Baltic states (Estonia, Latvia, Lithuania) declared independence in 1990; other republics followed through 1991
Failed coup attempt by Communist hardliners (August 19–22, 1991) backfired and accelerated dissolution
Belavezha Accords (December 8, 1991): Russia, Ukraine, Belarus declared the USSR dissolved; Commonwealth of Independent States formed
15 independent nations emerged; Russia inherited the USSR's UN Security Council seat, nuclear arsenal, and most international obligations
items.cardFront: December 25, 1991, Gorbachev resigns, USSR dissolves

items.prompt: Which genocide in 1994 saw Hutu extremists massacre approximately 800,000 Tutsi and moderate Hutu people in Rwanda over 100 days while the international community, including the UN, failed to intervene?
items.explanation:
April 7 – July 15, 1994; ~800,000 Tutsi and moderate Hutu killed in approximately 100 days
Triggered by the assassination of Hutu President Juvénal Habyarimana (plane shot down April 6, 1994)
Hutu extremist militias (Interahamwe) used machetes; Radio Mille Collines broadcast names of Tutsi targets
UN force commander General Roméo Dallaire warned the UN of the impending genocide weeks in advance; his requests for permission to act were denied
USA and Western powers deliberately avoided the word 'genocide' to sidestep legal obligation to intervene under the Genocide Convention
RPF (Rwandan Patriotic Front) under Paul Kagame stopped the genocide by July 1994; 2 million Hutus fled to Congo; Clinton later called inaction one of his greatest regrets
items.cardFront: 1994, Rwanda, ~800,000 Tutsi killed in 100 days

items.prompt: Which terrorist attacks on September 11, 2001, carried out by 19 al-Qaeda hijackers who crashed planes into the World Trade Center and the Pentagon, killed nearly 3,000 people and launched the U.S. 'War on Terror'?
items.explanation:
September 11, 2001; 19 al-Qaeda operatives hijacked four commercial aircraft
Two planes struck the World Trade Center in New York; both 110-story towers collapsed within 2 hours; ~2,600 killed
One plane hit the Pentagon in Arlington, Virginia; 184 killed
United 93 crashed in Shanksville, Pennsylvania after passengers revolted; 40 killed; intended target believed to be the Capitol or White House
Osama bin Laden of al-Qaeda claimed responsibility; killed by U.S. Navy SEALs in Abbottabad, Pakistan on May 2, 2011
Led directly to the invasion of Afghanistan (October 2001), the Iraq War (2003), the Department of Homeland Security, and the USA PATRIOT Act
items.cardFront: September 11, 2001, al-Qaeda, hijacked 4 planes, ~3,000 deaths
